module: Acute Kidney Injury Part III: Pathophysiology

questions.question: Regarding nephrotoxic AKI, which of the following are true?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Has distinct pathophysiology from other forms of AKI

statements.label: B
statements.text: Tubular toxicity is increased by filtration and concentration of circulating toxins

statements.label: C
statements.text: Radiological contrast exposure is the most significant nephrotoxic exposure in critically ill patients

statements.label: D
statements.text: >20% of the 100 drugs most frequently administered to adult ICU patients are classified as nephrotoxic

questions.explanation: Nephrotoxic AKI shares inflammatory pathways with other forms of AKI rather than having distinct pathophysiology. Tubular epithelial cells are especially prone to toxic injury as they concentrate filtered toxins and express specific transporters causing high intracellular uptake. The risk of contrast-induced AKI is smaller than previously suggested; aminoglycosides, vancomycin and NSAIDs are more significant nephrotoxic exposures in the ICU. Per Nash et al. (PMID:11979336), more than 20% of the 100 most frequently administered ICU drugs are nephrotoxic, underscoring the importance of medication review.
questions.topic: nephrotoxic AKI contrast aminoglycosides tubular toxicity
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part III: Pathophysiology

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology of AKI, which of the following is true?
options: a. Decrease in GFR is paralleled by proportionate increase in serum creatinine
b. Similar local inflammatory mechanism can mediate tubular injury in septic, post-surgical, ischaemic and nephrotoxic AKI
c. Severity of tubular injury has little effect on prognosis
d. Patterns of gene expression are similar across all causes of AKI
e. Transient reduction of renal blood flow to 10% of normal is sufficient to mediate sustained AKI in the absence of other risk factors
questions.explanation: Both innate and adaptive immune systems contribute to tubular injury regardless of aetiology — septic, post-surgical, ischaemic and nephrotoxic AKI share local inflammatory mechanisms involving immune cell infiltration. GFR and serum creatinine do not rise proportionately (creatinine is a delayed and insensitive marker). Severity of tubular injury does affect prognosis. Gene expression patterns differ by cause. Transient 10% RBF reduction alone does not sustain AKI without additional risk factors.
questions.topic: AKI pathophysiology inflammation tubular injury immune
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part III: Pathophysiology

questions.question: A 66-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after a hybrid repair of a complex aortic aneurysm. He has a history of type 2 DM with stage 3b chronic kidney disease (CKD). The following are risk factors for developing an AKI:
questions.explanation: All four are established risk factors for AKI. Advanced age, pre-existing CKD, radiocontrast agent exposure, and prolonged major non-cardiac surgery are all independently associated with post-operative AKI. The patient's combination of age, CKD stage 3b, intraoperative contrast, and complex vascular surgery places him at very high risk.
questions.topic: AKI risk factors CKD contrast surgery perioperative
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part III: Pathophysiology

questions.question: Regarding the pathophysiology of AKI, which of the following are true?

statements.label: A
statements.text: Tubular cells undergo cell division in response to injury

statements.label: C
statements.text: The thin loop is preferentially injured due to high oxygen demand

statements.label: D
statements.text: Increased afferent arteriolar resistance links tubular injury to loss of GFR

questions.explanation: In AKI the predominant pathology is tubular cell injury and dysfunction rather than frank cell division. Established AKI is characterised by low renal blood flow and HIGH intra-capsular pressure (not decreased). It is the distal proximal tubules and thick ascending limb (not the thin loop) that are at highest risk due to their high oxygen demand and ischaemic vulnerability. Afferent arteriole constriction reduces both RBF and glomerular capillary pressure in parallel, causing a marked fall in GFR. Note: ESICM feedback confirms this statement is false because it is the thick ascending limb, not the thin loop, that is preferentially injured.
questions.topic: AKI pathophysiology tubular injury GFR afferent arteriole oxygen demand
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part III: Pathophysiology

questions.question: AKI diagnosis and underlying renal pathology can poorly correlate as:

statements.label: A
statements.text: Tubular injury in AKI is indirectly related to changes in GFR

statements.label: B
statements.text: Urine output can be preserved or increased in moderate AKI

statements.label: C
statements.text: In most forms of AKI in the ICU tubular necrosis is rare and cellular changes on light microscopy are subtle

statements.label: D
statements.text: In septic shock patients, the main mechanism of AKI is prerenal

questions.explanation: The hallmark clinical diagnosis of AKI (declining GFR) is driven by tubular injury only indirectly — GFR reflects glomerular haemodynamics rather than tubular integrity. Urine output may indeed persist until renal function almost ceases, explaining poor correlation. Most ICU AKI involves tubular cell dysfunction without frank necrosis, so light microscopy is frequently unremarkable. The leading causes of ICU AKI are septic shock, major surgery and cardiogenic shock — in septic shock the mechanism is microvascular and inflammatory, NOT simply prerenal; this is a key conceptual distinction.
questions.topic: AKI diagnosis pathology correlation tubular necrosis sepsis prerenal
questions._module: Acute Kidney Injury Part III: Pathophysiology

questions.question: Regarding sepsis mediated AKI, which one of the following statements is most appropriate?
options: a. Toll-like receptors play an important role in the pathophysiology of injury
b. Renal blood flow is consistently decreased
c. Renal blood flow and GFR are strongly correlated
d. Other aetiologies of AKI rarely co-exist
e. Renal blood flow is consistently increased
questions.explanation: Toll-like receptors expressed on tubular and endothelial cells mediate the inflammatory response in sepsis-AKI; receptor activation triggers local pro-inflammatory mediator release and peritubular immune cell recruitment. Renal blood flow in sepsis is variable and not consistently decreased or increased — sepsis causes profound alterations in macro- and microcirculation with heterogeneity of regional flow and decreased functional capillary density, dissociating RBF from GFR. Multiple co-existing AKI mechanisms (nephrotoxins, hypoperfusion, obstruction) are the rule rather than the exception in ICU patients.
questions.topic: sepsis AKI toll-like receptors renal blood flow GFR microcirculation
